planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter while dormant; in zone 9 plant December–February. Orient pear is partially self-fertile and will produce some fruit without a cross-pollinator, but production increases substantially when planted with Kieffer, Moonglow, or another pear variety within 50 feet. Chill hour requirement is approximately 350–450 hours below 45°F, making it one of the more widely adapted pears for zone 9 inland gardens. Fruits ripen in August–September in zone 9.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, deep, slightly acidic to neutral soil — like the Kieffer, Orient pear tolerates the heavier clay soils common in the Gulf South better than most fruit trees; Orient is a Southern-bred pear hybrid (Pyrus communis x Pyrus pyrifolia) released by the Georgia Experiment Station in 1945, developed specifically for the heat, humidity, and disease pressure of the American Southeast. The large, golden-yellow, round to bell-shaped fruits are among the largest of any pear variety commonly grown in the South, with smooth, butter-textured, mildly sweet flesh that ripens earlier than Kieffer (August rather than September-October). Orient's exceptional resistance to fire blight — the bacterial disease that devastates pears in humid southern climates — was the primary breeding objective that made it a success in the Southeast. The combination of large fruit size, fire blight resistance, adequate flavor, and low chill hour requirement makes Orient one of the most reliably productive pears for zone 9 conditions in the inland South.
sources: University of Georgia Extension — Orient Pear for Southern Orchards
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Orient Pear Production Guide
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pear Variety Selection for Warm Climates

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; in zone 9 plant December–February. Chinese Apricot is self-fertile and does not require a cross-pollinator, though fruit size and yield improve with a nearby pollinizer. Chill hour requirement is approximately 400–500 hours, making it viable in the colder parts of zone 9. Late spring frost after bloom is the primary risk — site in a well-drained location with good cold air drainage and avoid frost pockets. Fruits ripen in May–June in zone 9.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil on a gently sloping site to encourage cold air drainage away from spring blossoms; Chinese Apricot (also known as 'Chinese Sweet' or 'Mormon Apricot') is an heirloom apricot variety valued for its early and reliable fruiting in warm-climate zones, exceptional ornamental spring bloom of pure white blossoms (among the most beautiful of any stone fruit), and large, orange-blushed fruits with rich, sweet, classic apricot flavor. The self-fertile habit — rare among apricots, which are often incompatible — makes Chinese Apricot practical for small gardens with space for only a single tree. The primary challenge in zone 9 for any apricot is that the early bloom (January–February) may be damaged by late frost; selecting a well-drained site with good cold air movement significantly reduces frost-damaged crop losses. Chinese Apricot has demonstrated better heat tolerance in warm-summer climates than most European apricot selections, contributing to its success in the South and Southwest.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Apricot Varieties for Texas
University of California Cooperative Extension — Apricot Variety Selection
Utah State University Extension — Chinese Apricot Culture

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Wizard Sunset coleus grows quickly and fills in beds and containers with brilliant warm-toned foliage. Pinch flower spikes as soon as they appear to maintain the foliage color and compact shape — flowering signals the end of the plant's productive foliage season. In zone 9 the long warm season allows extended growth; the plants can be maintained as large specimens through the year in frost-free areas.

directions: Plant in partial shade to shade in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Wizard Sunset is part of the Wizard coleus series — a compact, seed-grown coleus series known for uniform habit and a wide range of color selections. The Sunset color in the Wizard series features leaves in a warm blend of sunset orange, gold, red-orange, and bronze arranged in irregular patterns, capturing the warm glow of an evening sky. The compact, 12–18 inch height and dense branching habit of the Wizard series makes it one of the most practical seed-grown coleus for formal bedding patterns, edging, and container planting where a consistent, predictable size is required. Unlike the large-leaved vegetatively propagated coleus varieties, seed-grown Wizard types are uniform in size, color, and habit — each plant from the same packet is nearly identical, making them reliable for mass planting effects. In partial shade the warm sunset colors are often most vivid; deep shade tends to deepen the red tones while reducing the gold and orange.
sources: All-America Selections — Wizard Coleus Series Award
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Coleus for Shade Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Annual Foliage Plants for Gulf South

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May and again August–September for a fall harvest. Kaprao holy basil thrives in zone 9's heat and humidity — unlike sweet basil which may decline in extreme summer heat, holy basil actively thrives in tropical-humid conditions. In zone 9 Kaprao can be grown as a perennial in frost-free microclimates, returning from roots or self-seeding abundantly. The plants flower quickly in long summer days — pinch frequently to maintain vegetative growth if leaf production is the goal.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; Kaprao (กะเพรา in Thai, pronounced 'kra-pao') is the specific holy basil type used in Thai cooking — its clove-and-pepper-forward flavor with a faint anise note and spicy heat is the defining herb of pad kra pao (stir-fried basil), Thailand's most popular everyday dish. While all holy basil (Ocimum tenuiflorum) has the characteristic clove-spice aroma from eugenol, the Kaprao type selected for Thai cooking has a particularly pungent, peppery quality and narrow, slightly serrated leaves that are used exclusively in their cooked form — unlike Italian sweet basil or Vietnamese basil, Kaprao is wilted into hot wok dishes where the heat activates and transforms the volatile oils. The fresh leaves cannot be substituted by Italian sweet basil in pad kra pao, as the clove-pepper flavor profile is essential to the dish. In zone 9 Kaprao thrives in the hot, humid summers that are close to its native tropical Thai climate, performing far better than sweet basil in the season's peak heat.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Herbs for Florida Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Specialty Asian Herbs for Texas
Royal Flora Bangkok — Traditional Thai Culinary Herbs

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface (seeds need light to germinate); in zone 9 plant March–May and again August–September. Green shiso self-seeds prolifically — allow a few plants to go to seed at end of season and a permanent self-seeding colony will establish. In zone 9 the long warm season allows continuous leaf harvest from spring through early fall before the shorter days trigger flowering. Harvest young, large leaves frequently to stimulate continued growth; flowers and seeds are also edible and flavorful.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; Green Perilla Shiso (the green-leaved form of Perilla frutescens var. crispa) is the standard Japanese culinary shiso used extensively in Japanese cuisine — the large, ruffled, bright green leaves with their distinctive complex fragrance combining mint, anise, cinnamon, and citrus are served with sashimi, used to wrap sushi, sliced into cold noodle dishes, tempura-battered and fried, and used as a garnish for countless Japanese preparations. Green shiso is the preferred type for fresh-leaf use, having a brighter, more delicate flavor than red shiso (which is primarily used for coloring and flavoring pickled umeboshi plums). The large, attractive, deeply ruffled leaves have an impressive appearance for a kitchen herb, growing to 4–5 inches across on robust plants that do not require much care. Green shiso thrives in the warm, humid conditions of the Gulf South and tolerates some shade, making it practical for partially shaded kitchen herb gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Vegetables for Florida
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Specialty Asian Herbs
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Perilla Production for Asian Markets
